title: Buddhist and Jain Manuscripts
focus: Details the Vimalaprabha (Buddhist Tantra) and the Shantinatha Charitra (Jain Tirthankara biography), including their unique artistic features.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 019]
The Vimalaprabhā Laghukālacakratantrarājatīkā (Successfully inscribed in 2011)
* A palm-leaf manuscript, serving as the primary commentary on the Kalachakra Tantra, a key text in Vajrayana Buddhism.
* Tantra apart, the text contains detailed discussions of astrology and astronomy.
* Ayurveda occupies an important thematic focus in the Tantra.
* Many of Indian philosophical concepts are discussed in depth in this treatise.
* Measurements of the globe are given which are more detailed than those given in the Abhidharmakosa, a celebrated 4th Century text authored by Acharya Vasubandhu, which is itself a veritable encyclopaedia of information.

[Page 020]
The Shantinatha Charitra (Successfully inscribed in 2013):
* A text in Sanskrit written in Devanagari script.
* It describes the life and times of Shantinatha, the sixteenth Jain Tirthankara.
* This work was composed and written in the late fourteenth century 1396 C.E.
* This unique manuscript contains as many as 10 images of scenes or miniature paintings from the life of Shantinatha in the style of Jain paintings from Gujarat.
* The ink used in the manuscript is gum lampblack and white paint made from mineral silver.
